mnemonics.title: Types of Addressing Modes
mnemonics.content: IMR DIX BRS - Immediate, Register, Direct, Indexed, Base-register, Relative, Stack - A mnemonic to remember the main addressing modes.

mnemonics.title: RISC Characteristics
mnemonics.content: LISP FCP: Load-Store, Indexed simple, Single-cycle, Fixed-length, Complex made simple, Pipelining-friendly - Remember key RISC features.

mnemonics.title: RISC vs CISC Difference
mnemonics.content: RISC = Reduced (simple), CISC = Complex. RISC does LESS in each instruction but FASTER; CISC does MORE but potentially SLOWER per instruction.

acronyms.usage: Processor design using small, optimized instruction set with load-store architecture (e.g., MIPS, ARM)
Processor design using large instruction set with complex operations (e.g., x86, VAX)
Register that holds address of next instruction to be fetched; used in relative addressing
Register pointing to top of stack; used in stack addressing mode
The actual memory address where the operand is located after address calculation

visualTips.visualization: Think of array indexing like a row of lockers. Base address is the first locker, index is which locker to open (0=first, 1=second). EA = First Locker + Locker Number.
Like a house address that points to another address. If address 500 says 'Go to address 700', the actual data is at 700. It's a pointer chain.
Like giving directions '3 blocks ahead'. PC shows current position, displacement is '3 blocks', destination is PC + 3 blocks.
Like an assembly line in a factory. Each instruction goes through stages (Fetch, Decode, Execute, Memory, Write). While one instruction is in stage 2, next is in stage 1.

analogies.analogy: Like writing a phone number directly on a form - the number is right there in your hand (instruction contains the value directly).
Like looking at a sticky note on your desk - you know exactly where to find the information (in the register).
RISC is like having simple tools (screwdriver, hammer) that do one job well. CISC is like a Swiss Army knife with many tools - more complex but can do more in one action.
Think of a stack of plates in a cafeteria. You can only add/remove from the top. The Stack Pointer always points to the top plate (most recent item).
